masthead.dek: Twenty questions, and the answer is almost never the first name that springs to mind. Each routes to a set you can walk — a championship venue, a country, a U.S. state, a guessable count — with a filter or a twist that knocks out the obvious. Narrow the field, place your bet, then tap to check.
masthead.footer_html: Twenty questions across venues, countries, states, records and counts — every fact web-verified and sourced.

plan.note: Answers routed to right-sized sets (US Open/Open venues ~50, countries ~100, US states 50, guessable counts) — never 'the four majors' as the answer set. Reflex-defeaters and slicers throughout (highest scoring average -> Winged Foot not Oakmont; per-capita -> North Dakota not Florida; 2nd/3rd/4th-place slicers).

questions.q: Of all the courses that have hosted the U.S. Open, which one saw the highest scoring average in a championship — a 1974 event so brutal it earned a nickname for the carnage?
questions.detail: The 1974 'Massacre at Winged Foot' produced a 76.73 field scoring average, the highest in U.S. Open history; Hale Irwin won at 7-over 287.
questions.why: No player broke par in any of the four rounds, a feat unmatched by other venues (76.73 average).
questions.more: Irwin's 7-over winning total is among the highest in relation to par at any major since 1963.
questions.source: SI.com, 'Five Most Grueling U.S. Opens'; golf records.

questions.q: Besides the United States, which country has produced the most men's major championship winners, owed largely to the game's birthplace heritage?
questions.detail: Scotland's golfers have won about 55 men's major titles, second only to the U.S. and ahead of England.
questions.why: As golf's home, Scotland's haul of roughly 55 majors tops every nation but the U.S.
questions.more: England is next with about 34 major titles.
questions.source: golf.org.uk; thegolfnewsnet major-champions-by-country.

questions.q: Who shot the lowest single round in PGA Tour history, a 58 at the 2016 Travelers Championship?
questions.detail: Jim Furyk shot 58 (12-under) in the final round of the 2016 Travelers at TPC River Highlands, with 10 birdies and an eagle.
questions.why: His 58 is the only one in Tour history and beat the prior record by a stroke.
questions.more: Furyk is the only player with two sub-60 rounds on Tour, having also carded a 59 in 2013.
questions.source: Guinness World Records; Wikipedia, 'Jim Furyk's round of 58'.

questions.q: New York has hosted the most U.S. Opens. Which state ranks second, thanks to storied courses like Oakmont and Merion?
questions.detail: Pennsylvania has hosted the U.S. Open 17 times, second only to New York's 20.
questions.why: Its cluster of championship courses puts it ahead of California for second place (17).
questions.more: California is third with 14 hostings.
questions.source: Stacker, 'States that have hosted the most US Opens'.

questions.q: When golf returned to the Olympics in 2016, who won the men's gold medal — the first awarded in the sport in over a century?
questions.detail: Justin Rose of Great Britain won gold at the 2016 Rio Games, two shots clear of Henrik Stenson.
questions.why: He was the first men's Olympic golf champion since 1904 — a 112-year gap.
questions.more: Rose also made the first hole-in-one in Olympic golf history during the event.
questions.source: Wikipedia, 'Golf at the 2016 Summer Olympics — Men's individual'.

questions.q: Which course has hosted The Open Championship more times than any other, fitting as the spiritual home of golf?
questions.detail: The Old Course at St. Andrews has hosted The Open about 30 times, more than any other venue, and returns in 2027 for the 31st.
questions.why: No venue is close to its hosting total in the championship's history.
questions.more: The 2027 Open will be its 31st time staging the event.
questions.source: theopen.com 'St Andrews 2027'; fansided St. Andrews history.

questions.q: Counting every staging since 1927, which side has won the Ryder Cup outright the most times overall?
questions.detail: The U.S. has won the Ryder Cup 27 times to Europe/Great Britain's 16, with 2 ties.
questions.why: American dominance from 1927 to 1983 built a lead Europe hasn't overtaken (27 wins).
questions.more: Since 1985, Europe has flipped it, winning 13 of 20 stagings.
questions.source: topendsports Ryder Cup winners; Wikipedia Ryder Cup records.

questions.q: At The Open Championship, the record low score relative to par over 72 holes is how many under — a mark Cameron Smith matched at St Andrews in 2022?
questions.detail: The Open's 72-hole record is 20-under, set by Henrik Stenson in 2016 and equalled by Cameron Smith in 2022.
questions.why: No Open winner has bettered 20-under in the championship's history.
questions.more: The overall major record is 21-under, set by Xander Schauffele at the 2024 PGA.
questions.source: Guinness World Records, 'Lowest score to par in The Open (72 holes)'.

questions.q: Under the rules of golf, a ball can't weigh more than how many ounces — a limit meant to cap how far it flies?
questions.detail: USGA rules cap a golf ball at 1.620 ounces (45.93 g); there is no minimum weight.
questions.why: Heavier balls fly farther, so the rule sets a ceiling at 1.62 ounces.
questions.more: The ball must also be at least 1.68 inches in diameter.
questions.source: USGA Appendix III; golfballs.com USGA regulations.

questions.q: Who came from ten shots back on the final day to win a major — the largest last-round comeback in major history, at Carnoustie in 1999?
questions.detail: Paul Lawrie trailed by 10 entering the final round of the 1999 Open and won a playoff over Van de Velde and Leonard.
questions.why: His 10-shot final-round comeback is the biggest in major championship history.
questions.more: Ranked 241st in the world, he was the first qualifier to win The Open since 1963.
questions.source: BBC Sport; Paisley Scotland, '25 years since Paul Lawrie's win'.

questions.q: Behind the U.S. and Scotland, which country sits third for most men's major titles — home to stars from Faldo to Rose?
questions.detail: England ranks third with about 34 men's major championship titles, behind the U.S. and Scotland.
questions.why: Its tally of roughly 34 majors edges out South Africa for third place.
questions.more: South Africa is next with about 22 major titles.
questions.source: golf.org.uk; pressreader South Africa majors.

questions.q: Which course played as the longest in major championship history when it hosted the 2017 U.S. Open in Wisconsin?
questions.detail: Erin Hills was set at a record 7,741 yards for the 2017 U.S. Open, the longest major venue ever.
questions.why: No major course had been stretched longer than Erin Hills' setup (7,741 yards).
questions.more: Brooks Koepka won that week at 16-under, tying the U.S. Open scoring record.
questions.source: CBS Sports; Wikipedia '2017 U.S. Open'.

questions.q: Who became the oldest man to win a major, capturing the 2021 PGA Championship at age 50?
questions.detail: Phil Mickelson won the 2021 PGA at Kiawah's Ocean Course at 50 years, 11 months — the oldest major champion ever.
questions.why: He broke a record that had stood since 1968.
questions.more: He topped Julius Boros, who won the 1968 PGA at age 48.
questions.source: PGA.com; ESPN, 'Phil Mickelson oldest major champion'.

questions.q: Which player tops the PGA Tour's all-time career money list, having earned well over $120 million on tour?
questions.detail: Tiger Woods leads the all-time PGA Tour money list with about $120.9 million in official earnings.
questions.why: No one else is close to his career on-course total (about $121 million).
questions.more: His 2000 season alone would be worth far more at today's purses.
questions.source: PGA Tour stats; all-time money list.

questions.q: A full regulation 18-hole course is most commonly designed to a par of what number?
questions.detail: The standard par for a regulation 18-hole course is 72 — typically four par-3s, ten par-4s, and four par-5s.
questions.why: It's the most common design total, though courses range from about 70 to 72.
questions.more: That layout usually splits into two par-3s and two par-5s per nine.
questions.source: Wikipedia 'Par (score)'; Under Armour 'What Is Par in Golf'.

questions.q: Which sparsely populated northern Plains state has the most golf courses per person of any U.S. state?
questions.detail: North Dakota leads in golf courses per capita, roughly one course for every 6,400 residents.
questions.why: Its small population spread over many small-town courses tops the per-capita list.
questions.more: Neighboring South Dakota is close with one course per about 7,300 people.
questions.source: Islands.com; GolfBlogger 'Golf Courses Per Capita'.

questions.q: Outside of the putter, the rules cap a golf club's overall length at how many inches?
questions.detail: USGA Rule 4.1b limits a club (excluding the putter) to 48 inches in overall length.
questions.why: That ceiling has long governed general play at 48 inches.
questions.more: Since 2022 elite events may use a local rule shortening it to 46 inches.
questions.source: USGA Rule 4.1b; golf.com 'maximum club length'.

questions.q: Which country, home to Player, Els, and Goosen, ranks fourth all-time in men's major titles despite its modest size?
questions.detail: South Africa ranks fourth with about 22 men's major championship titles, behind the U.S., Scotland, and England.
questions.why: Its roughly 22 majors are remarkable for a country its size, placing it fourth.
questions.more: That total trails England's 34 for third place.
questions.source: pressreader 'SA's success at Majors'; golf.org.uk.

questions.q: Florida has the most golf courses of any state. Which large western state ranks second?
questions.detail: California has the second-most golf courses of any U.S. state, trailing only Florida.
questions.why: Its size and climate put it just behind Florida for total courses.
questions.more: North Dakota, by contrast, leads only on a per-capita basis.
questions.source: World Population Review 'Golf Courses by State'; LINKS Magazine.

questions.q: Which New York course was the first publicly owned, government-run track to host a U.S. Open, doing so in 2002?
questions.detail: Bethpage Black, a state-park course in Farmingdale, hosted the 2002 U.S. Open — the first publicly owned course to do so.
questions.why: Every prior U.S. Open host had been a private or resort club.
questions.more: Tiger Woods won that week by three over Phil Mickelson.
questions.source: Wikipedia '2002 U.S. Open'; Golf Monthly 'Past Winners at Bethpage Black'.
